metadata.title: Multi-Provider Auth Backend & Rate Limit Tuning

story.iWant: to log in with any of my OAuth providers and reach the same account
story.soThat: I don't end up with duplicate accounts or lose access to my data

tasks.task: Create migration 000015_multi_provider_auth (drop UNIQUE(user_id), add UNIQUE(user_id, auth_provider), create user_refresh_tokens table, migrate data, drop old columns)
Update UserAuth model — remove RefreshToken and RefreshTokenExpiresAt fields
Refactor CreateOrUpdateOAuthUser() — insert new auth rows per provider instead of ON CONFLICT overwrite
Add UpsertRefreshToken() and GetRefreshToken() to user repository using user_refresh_tokens table
Update RefreshToken handler to use new table
Update Login handler — query users_auth WHERE auth_provider = 'local' for password check
Update OAuthLogin handler to work with refactored repository
Increase authIPRateLimiter limit from 10 to 20, create refreshIPRateLimiter with limit 30
Create RateLimitRefreshIP() middleware, move refresh_token route to separate rate limit group
Update existing auth tests, add new multi-provider and rate limit tests

acceptanceCriteria.ac: Given a user registered with email/password, when they log in with GitHub OAuth (same email), then a new users_auth row is created for GitHub AND the existing password auth row is preserved.
Given a user who previously logged in with GitHub, when they log in with Google (same email), then both GitHub and Google auth rows exist for the same user.
Given a user re-logs in with the same OAuth provider, when the callback hits the backend, then the existing auth row is updated (avatar, name) — no duplicate row is created.
Given the refresh token endpoint is called, when the user has a valid refresh token, then it reads from the user_refresh_tokens table (not users_auth).
Given the rate limit on auth endpoints, when a user makes 20 requests within 1 minute, then the 21st is rejected with 429 — and refresh token has its own separate 30/min bucket.
All existing auth tests pass with updated multi-provider behavior. New tests cover multi-provider scenarios.

doc.title: Tech Spec: Multi-Provider Auth & Rate Limit Tuning
doc.section: Implementation Details — Database Migration, Technical Approach, Rate Limit Implementation
doc.snippet: Complete migration SQL (DDL), refactored OAuth login flow pseudocode, rate limit configuration changes, data model before/after diagrams. Primary context document for this epic.

doc.title: Epic 8: Multi-Provider Authentication & Account Security
doc.snippet: Epic breakdown with 3 stories (8.1 backend core, 8.2 account management, 8.3 frontend). Story 8.1 is foundational — no forward dependencies.

doc.title: Backend Architecture
doc.section: Layered Architecture, Middleware, Error Handling, Testing Patterns
doc.snippet: Go/Gin backend follows handler → repository → model pattern with dependency injection via AppState. Rate limiting uses IP-based in-memory limiter for public endpoints.

doc.title: Database Schema
doc.snippet: Current schema: users (id, email UNIQUE, name, timestamps, soft delete) and users_auth (id, user_id UNIQUE, password_hash nullable, auth_provider, avatar_url, refresh_token, timestamps).

file.reason: The UNIQUE(user_id) constraint being dropped — this enforces one-to-one that must become one-to-many
Original table definitions — users_auth has refresh_token and refresh_token_expires_at columns that will be extracted to new table
UserAuth struct must be updated: remove RefreshToken and RefreshTokenExpiresAt fields. Add new UserRefreshToken struct.
Primary refactor target — ON CONFLICT (user_id) DO UPDATE must become check-and-insert per provider
Currently returns single auth row — Login handler needs to query by (user_id, auth_provider='local') instead
All refresh token methods must be rewritten to use user_refresh_tokens table instead of users_auth
Used by CreateOrUpdateOAuthUser — no changes needed, just reference for flow understanding
Calls CreateOrUpdateOAuthUser and UpdateRefreshToken — must work with refactored repository
Calls GetUserAuth(user.ID) — must change to query by (user_id, auth_provider='local') for password verification
Calls ValidateRefreshToken and UpdateRefreshToken — must use new user_refresh_tokens table methods
Creates initial users_auth row with auth_provider='local' — verify it still works with new composite unique constraint
Request/response types — no changes expected but referenced by handlers
authIPRateLimiter.limit must change from 10 to 20. New refreshIPRateLimiter with limit 30 and RateLimitRefreshIP() function needed.
refresh_token route must move from rateLimited group to new refreshLimited group with RateLimitRefreshIP()
Provider constants — no changes needed, reference for provider name strings
Existing tests must be updated for multi-provider behavior. Test setup manually adds UNIQUE(user_id) at line 42 — must be updated to match new constraint.
Repository tests verify OAuth user creation and refresh token operations — must be updated for multi-provider and new table.
Reference only — frontend calls POST /api/oauth and POST /api/refresh_token. No frontend changes in Story 8.1, but backend must remain compatible with these calls.

constraints.constraint: Follow existing handler pattern: struct with NewXHandler(appState) constructor, ShouldBindJSON + validator.Struct for request parsing
Follow existing repository pattern: raw SQL with sqlx.Get/Select/Exec, db:"column" tags on models
Error handling: use pkg/errors package (ConvertError, NewDatabaseError, IsNotFoundError)
Migration files: sequential numbered (000015), separate up/down SQL files
Test pattern: table-driven tests with testify (require.NoError, assert.Equal), test DB with testutil helpers
Migration must be backward-compatible: existing data must survive (refresh tokens migrated, auth rows preserved)
Frontend compatibility: POST /api/oauth and POST /api/refresh_token request/response format must not change

interface.name: POST /api/oauth
interface.signature: POST /api/oauth {provider: string, email: string, name: string, avatar_url?: string} → {success: true, data: {user: User, access_token: string, refresh_token: string, expires_in: int}}

interface.name: POST /api/refresh_token
interface.signature: POST /api/refresh_token {refresh_token: string} → {success: true, data: {access_token: string, refresh_token: string, expires_in: int}}

interface.name: POST /api/login
interface.signature: POST /api/login {email: string, password: string} → {success: true, data: AuthResponse}

interface.name: POST /api/users (Register)
interface.signature: POST /api/users {email: string, name: string, password: string} → {success: true, data: AuthResponse}

interface.name: CreateOrUpdateOAuthUser
interface.signature: func (r *UserRepository) CreateOrUpdateOAuthUser(email, name, provider, avatarURL string) (*models.User, error)

interface.name: UpdateRefreshToken
interface.signature: func (r *UserRepository) UpdateRefreshToken(userID uuid.UUID, refreshToken string, expiresAt time.Time) error

interface.name: ValidateRefreshToken
interface.signature: func (r *UserRepository) ValidateRefreshToken(userID uuid.UUID, refreshToken string) (bool, error)

tests.standards: Backend uses Go testing package with testify v1.10.0 (require for fatal, assert for non-fatal). Table-driven tests are the standard pattern. Test database is set up via testutil helpers with fresh migrations. Test file naming: *_test.go in the same package. Test functions: TestXxx with subtests via t.Run("SubtestName", ...). Auth tests manually add UNIQUE constraint at setup (line 42 of auth_test.go) — this must be updated to reflect the new composite unique constraint.

ideas.idea: Test: register with email/password, then OAuth login with same email (GitHub) → verify 2 auth rows exist, password hash preserved
Test: OAuth login with GitHub, then OAuth login with Google (same email) → verify both auth rows, same user ID
Test: OAuth login with GitHub twice → verify only 1 GitHub auth row, avatar_url updated
Test: generate refresh token → verify stored in user_refresh_tokens table → call refresh endpoint → verify new token returned from new table
Test: refresh token rotation — old token invalidated after refresh
Test: 20 auth requests in 1 min → 21st returns 429 with correct headers
Test: auth rate limit and refresh rate limit are independent — hitting auth limit doesn't block refresh
Test: all existing OAuth tests pass (new user, existing user, all providers, invalid provider, missing fields)
Test: all existing refresh token tests pass with new table backend
Test: register with password, OAuth with GitHub, OAuth with Google — verify 3 auth rows (local, github, google) for same user
